questionText: Rocky can walk a certain distance in 40 days when he rests 9 hours a day. How long will he take to walk twice the distance, twice as fast and rest twice as long each day?

steps.explanation: Identify the three factors and their proportionality type. Let the original distance be $y$, original speed be $z$, original rest time be 9 hours, original days = 40. New distance = $2y$, new speed = $2z$, new rest time = 18 hours, required days = $x$.
More distance → more days (Direct Proportion). Set up ratio: $y : 2y$ contributes factor $\frac{2y}{y} = 2$ to days.
More speed → fewer days (Indirect Proportion). Set up ratio: $2z : z$ contributes factor $\frac{z}{2z} = \frac{1}{2}$ to days.
More resting time → more days (Direct Proportion). Set up ratio: $9 : 18$ contributes factor $\frac{18}{9} = 2$ to days.
Multiply all factors with original days to get required days.

solution.commonMistake: Treating 'more speed' as direct proportion (it is indirect — faster speed means fewer days).

questionText: 15 persons working 8 hours a day can complete a work in 21 days. How many days will 14 persons take to complete a work $1\frac{1}{2}$ times as great, if they work 6 hours a day?

steps.explanation: Identify all factors. Original: 15 persons, 8 hrs/day, 21 days, work = $y$. New: 14 persons, 6 hrs/day, $x$ days, work = $\frac{3}{2}y$.
Fewer persons → more days (Indirect). Factor from persons: $\frac{15}{14}$.
Fewer hours per day → more days (Indirect). Factor from hours: $\frac{8}{6}$.
More work → more days (Direct). Factor from work: $\frac{3/2}{1} = \frac{3}{2}$.
Multiply all factors with original days.

solution.commonMistake: Confusing '1½ times as great' with '½ times as great'; it means work = $\frac{3}{2}$ of original.

formulas.title: Work-Men-Days-Hours relation
formulas.expression: M_1 \times D_1 \times H_1 \times W_2 = M_2 \times D_2 \times H_2 \times W_1

questionText: A contract is to be completed in 50 days and 105 men were set to work, each working 8 hours a day. After 25 days, $\frac{2}{5}$ of the work is finished. How many additional men should be employed so that the work may be completed on time, each man now working 9 hours a day?

steps.explanation: After 25 days, $\frac{2}{5}$ of work is done. Remaining work $= 1 - \frac{2}{5} = \frac{3}{5}$. Remaining time $= 50 - 25 = 25$ days. Let additional men $= x$, so total men now $= 105 + x$.

Set up chain rule: Days: $25:25$ (same days, indirect → factor $\frac{25}{25}=1$). Hours: $9:8$ (more hours → less men, indirect → factor $\frac{8}{9}$). Work: $\frac{2}{5} : \frac{3}{5}$ (more work → more men, direct → factor $\frac{3/5}{2/5} = \frac{3}{2}$).
Apply the chain rule to find $105 + x$.
Verify using the direct equation: $25 \times 9 \times \frac{2}{5} \times (105+x) = 25 \times 8 \times \frac{3}{5} \times 105$.
Additional men required = $140 - 105 = 35$.

solution.shortcut: Compute remaining work and remaining time first, then apply chain rule with adjusted hours.
solution.commonMistake: Using total days (50) instead of remaining days (25) in the chain rule setup.

questionText: If 9 engines consume 24 metric tonnes of coal, when each is working 8 hours a day; how much coal will be required for 8 engines, each running 13 hours a day, it being given that 3 engines of the former type consume as much as 4 engines of the latter type?

steps.explanation: Determine relative consumption rates. 3 former engines consume as much as 4 latter engines in 1 hour. So 1 former engine consumes $\frac{1}{3}$ unit/hour and 1 latter engine consumes $\frac{1}{4}$ unit/hour.
Fewer engines → less coal (Direct). Factor from engines: $\frac{8}{9}$.
More working hours → more coal (Direct). Factor from hours: $\frac{13}{8}$.
Less rate of consumption per engine (latter type consumes $\frac{1}{4}$ vs former's $\frac{1}{3}$) → less coal. Factor from rate: $\frac{1/4}{1/3} = \frac{3}{4}$.
Apply all factors to original coal consumption.

solution.shortcut: The rate ratio $\frac{1/4}{1/3} = \frac{3}{4}$ accounts for different engine efficiencies in one step.
solution.commonMistake: Ignoring the differing consumption rates between the two types of engines.

questionText: A garrison of 3300 men had provisions for 32 days, when given at the rate of 850 gms per head. At the end of 7 days, a reinforcement arrives and it was found that the provisions will last 17 days more, when given at the rate of 825 gms per head. What is the strength of the reinforcement?

steps.explanation: After 7 days, the remaining provisions are enough for $(32 - 7) = 25$ days for 3300 men at 850 gms/head. We need to find how many men (at 825 gms/head) these provisions will last for 17 days.
Less ration per head → more men (Indirect). Factor from ration: $\frac{850}{825}$.
Fewer days → more men (Indirect). Factor from days: $\frac{25}{17}$.
Total men after reinforcement:
Strength of reinforcement = Total men after − Original garrison.

solution.commonMistake: Using 32 days instead of the remaining 25 days after 7 days have passed.

formulas.title: Provisions formula
formulas.expression: M_1 \times D_1 \times R_1 = M_2 \times D_2 \times R_2

questionText: Two coal loading machines each working 12 hours per day for 8 days handle 9000 tonnes of coal with an efficiency of 90%. Three other coal loading machines at an efficiency of 80% are set to handle 12000 tonnes of coal in 6 days. Find how many hours per day each should work.

steps.explanation: Identify the four factors. Original: 2 machines, 12 hrs/day, 8 days, 9000 tonnes, 90% efficiency. New: 3 machines, $x$ hrs/day, 6 days, 12000 tonnes, 80% efficiency.
More machines → fewer hours/day (Indirect). Factor: $\frac{2}{3}$.
Fewer days → more hours/day (Indirect). Factor: $\frac{8}{6}$.
More coal → more hours/day (Direct). Factor: $\frac{12000}{9000} = \frac{4}{3}$.
Less efficiency → more hours/day (Indirect). Factor: $\frac{90}{80} = \frac{9}{8}$.
Multiply all factors with original hours per day.

solution.shortcut: Set up the combined equation: $3 \times 6 \times 9000 \times 80 \times x = 2 \times 8 \times 12000 \times 90 \times 12$ and solve for $x$.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing efficiency as direct proportion — lower efficiency means more hours are needed (indirect).
